one_line_takeaway: Recasting machine unlearning as a data-attribution problem, DMM fine-tunes a model to match datamodel-simulated 'retrain-without-the-forget-set' outputs, beating prior gradient-based unlearners on a privacy-grounded KLoM metric at under 5% of retraining compute.
key_finding: DMM (and the idealized DM-DIRECT) dominate the Pareto frontier of prior unlearning methods and approach oracle-retraining quality at a fraction of the compute. Oracle Matching achieves effective unlearning using less than 5% of full-retraining compute, whereas matching OM's forget-set performance via partial retraining requires more than 60% of full-retraining time. OM is stable over training time (once a point is unlearned it stays unlearned, avoiding the overshoot seen with SCRUB) and generalizes from a small retain sample (a retain fraction >= 0.04 suffices). Modeling cross-example (off-diagonal) datamodel interactions is necessary for non-random forget sets; diagonal-only memorization scores fail there even after global rescaling. In the convex analysis, including forget-set points in the objective is the key driver of OM's advantage, and Theorem 2 shows stochastic OM converges exponentially fast while SGD has an Omega(1/t^2) lower bound.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Datamodel Matching (DMM), a meta-algorithm for approximate machine unlearning that reduces unlearning to predictive data attribution (datamodeling). Its own contribution has three parts: (1) Oracle Matching (OM), a fine-tuning primitive that, given oracle outputs from a model retrained without the forget set, minimizes MSE between the current model's logits and those oracle outputs on a sampled fine-tune set (forget set plus a small retain subset), sidestepping the gradient-ascent 'missing targets' problem; (2) replacing the unavailable oracle with simulated outputs from a (linear) datamodel via DM-DIRECT, which estimates the counterfactual output as f_x(theta) minus the sum of datamodel coefficients over the forget set; (3) DMM, which runs OM on the datamodel-simulated oracle to unlearn in weight space. The authors also contribute a new evaluation metric, KLoM (KL divergence of Margins), and a convex-case (under-determined ridge regression) theory showing OM/DMM advantages. The reduction is the headline claim: future advances in data attribution translate directly into better unlearning.
threat_model: Genuine and drawn from the paper, not imposed. Unlearning is motivated by privacy/deletion requirements (EU Right to be Forgotten, removing toxic/poisoned/copyrighted data, LLM alignment). The formal target is (epsilon, delta)-approximate unlearning (Definition 2), intentionally mirroring differential privacy: the distribution of unlearned models must be statistically indistinguishable from models retrained without the forget set, so an adversary observing the returned model cannot infer forget-set membership much better than against a fully retrained model. Evaluation operationalizes this adversary via U-LiRA, a membership-inference-style test (from Hayes et al., inspired by Carlini et al. MIAs) that asks an optimal likelihood-ratio adversary to distinguish forget-set points from held-out validation points by their output margins. The paper's own KLoM metric measures the KL divergence between unlearned-model margins and a reference 'safe' distribution (retrained models).
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) methods are validated only on classification with few classes; extending to many classes (full ImageNet, language modeling) would require attributing all class logits at heavy computational cost. (2) OM/DMM can mismatch the oracle on the tail of the retain/validation sets, attributed to gradient updates 'reversing' original-training overfitting. (3) Even the most efficient data-attribution estimators cost on the order of training the original model, and the reported fine-tuning compute excludes the one-time datamodel-estimation cost (standard in the unlearning literature but a real practical burden). (4) Only single unlearning requests are studied; sequential DMM may require recalibrating datamodels and its composition behavior is untested. Reviewer note: the convergence theory covers only under-determined ridge regression, not the deep networks where the empirical wins are claimed.

datasets_used: Synthetic under-determined ridge regression (n=100, d=400, forget set |S_F|=5)
quantitative_results: Oracle Matching reaches effective unlearning using <5% of full-retraining compute
Matching OM's forget-set unlearning quality via retraining requires >60% of full-retraining time
Evaluation uses 100 unlearned models and 100 retrained oracle models per method; KLoM reported at 95th percentile per split plus average
Retain-set sampling fraction >= 0.04 suffices for OM to generalize
Datamodel estimation cost swept over {10^3, 10^4, 2x10^4, 4x10^4} retrained models; KLoM saturates while LDS keeps improving
TRAK-based DMM uses ~1000x less compute than regression datamodels and still beats all prior methods despite worse KLoM
Convex study: 24% of forget-set mass lies in the retain-set span, but that span holds <0.01% of the ground-truth update theta*-theta_full
Theorem 2: stochastic OM converges at linear rate O((1-gamma_min*eta)^t); SGD has Omega(1/t^2) lower bound
baselines_compared: Gradient Descent + Ascent (GDA) and Oracle Matching on Retain Set (OMRS) in the convex study
DM-DIRECT and DMM(TRAK) as the paper's own variants
